commentaries.author: Augustine of Hippo
commentaries.content:
Whereas that life is the light of men, but foolish hearts cannot receive that light, beingso encumbered with sins that they cannot see it; for this cause lest any should think there is no light near them, because they cannot see it, he continues: And the light shines in darkness, and the darkness comprehended it not. For suppose a blind man standing in the sun, the sun is present to him, but he is absent from the sun. In like manner every fool is blind, and wisdomis present to him; but, though present, absent from his sight, forasmuch as sight is gone: the truth being, not that she is absent from him, but that he is absent from her.
A certain Platonist once said, that the beginning of this Gospel ought to be copied in letters of gold, and placed in the most conspicuous place in every church.
commentaries.content_en:
Whereas that life is the light of men, but foolish hearts cannot receive that light, beingso encumbered with sins that they cannot see it; for this cause lest any should think there is no light near them, because they cannot see it, he continues: And the light shines in darkness, and the darkness comprehended it not. For suppose a blind man standing in the sun, the sun is present to him, but he is absent from the sun. In like manner every fool is blind, and wisdomis present to him; but, though present, absent from his sight, forasmuch as sight is gone: the truth being, not that she is absent from him, but that he is absent from her.
A certain Platonist once said, that the beginning of this Gospel ought to be copied in letters of gold, and placed in the most conspicuous place in every church.
commentaries.content_pt:
Considerando que essa vida é a luz dos homens, mas corações insensatos não podem receber essa luz, estando tão sobrecarregados com pecados que não conseguem vê-la; por essa razão, para que ninguém pense que não há luz próxima a eles, porque não conseguem vê-la, ele continua: E a luz brilha nas trevas, e as trevas não a compreenderam. Pois suponha um cego em pé ao sol, o sol está presente a ele, mas ele está ausente do sol. Da mesma forma, todo tolo é cego, e a sabedoria está presente a ele; mas, embora presente, ausente de sua vista, uma vez que a visão se foi: a verdade é que não está ausente dele, mas ele está ausente dela.
Um certo platonista disse uma vez que o início deste Evangelho deveria ser copiado em letras de ouro e colocado no lugar mais conspícuo de cada igreja.

commentaries.author: Bede
commentaries.content: The other Evangelists describe Christ as born in time; John witnesses that He was in the beginning, saying, In the beginning was the Word. The others describe His sudden appearance among men; he witnesses that He was ever with God, saying, And the Word was with God. The others prove Him very man; he very God, saying, And the Word was God. The others exhibit Him as man conversing with men for a season; he pronounces Him God abiding with God in the beginning, saying, The Same was in the beginning with God. The others relate the great deeds which He did amongst men; he that God the Father made every creature through Him, saying, All things were made by Him, and without Him was not any shiny made.
commentaries.content_en: The other Evangelists describe Christ as born in time; John witnesses that He was in the beginning, saying, In the beginning was the Word. The others describe His sudden appearance among men; he witnesses that He was ever with God, saying, And the Word was with God. The others prove Him very man; he very God, saying, And the Word was God. The others exhibit Him as man conversing with men for a season; he pronounces Him God abiding with God in the beginning, saying, The Same was in the beginning with God. The others relate the great deeds which He did amongst men; he that God the Father made every creature through Him, saying, All things were made by Him, and without Him was not any shiny made.
commentaries.content_pt: Os outros Evangelistas descrevem Cristo como nascido no tempo; João testemunha que Ele estava no princípio, dizendo: No princípio era o Verbo. Os outros descrevem Sua súbita aparição entre os homens; ele testemunha que Ele sempre esteve com Deus, dizendo: E o Verbo estava com Deus. Os outros provam que Ele é verdadeiramente homem; ele, verdadeiramente Deus, dizendo: E o Verbo era Deus. Os outros o exibem como homem conversando com homens por um tempo; ele o pronuncia Deus permanecendo com Deus no princípio, dizendo: Este estava no princípio com Deus. Os outros relatam as grandes obras que Ele fez entre os homens; ele que Deus o Pai fez toda criatura através d'Ele, dizendo: Todas as coisas foram feitas por Ele, e sem Ele nada do que foi feito se fez.

commentaries.author: Clement Of Alexandria
commentaries.content:
But he who has the light watches, "and darkness seizes not on him".
For how, says Heraclitus, can one escape the notice of that which never sets? Let us by no means, then, veil our selves with the darkness; for the light dwells in us. "For the darkness "it is said, "comprehendeth it not."
commentaries.content_en:
But he who has the light watches, "and darkness seizes not on him".
For how, says Heraclitus, can one escape the notice of that which never sets? Let us by no means, then, veil our selves with the darkness; for the light dwells in us. "For the darkness "it is said, "comprehendeth it not."
commentaries.content_pt:
Mas aquele que tem a luz vigia, "e as trevas não se apoderam dele".
Pois como, diz Heráclito, pode alguém escapar da atenção daquilo que nunca se põe? Não devemos, portanto, de forma alguma, nos cobrir com as trevas; pois a luz habita em nós. "Pois as trevas", diz-se, "não a compreendem."

commentaries.author: Cyril of Alexandria
commentaries.content: Needs does the most wise Evangelist hasten to expand to us by this too that is now before us the thought expressed above. For he did not think, I suppose, that it would suffice to the hearers unto being able to think unerringly of God the Word, that He is verily the Light of men, by only saying, And the Life was the Light of men. For it was like I suppose that some would arise who should hear the things uttered without weighing them, and should moreover set forth or try to teach others also that the Word of God is indeed verily Light, but not Giver of light to all, but in whomsoever He will He infuses the light of understanding, approving him who |68 ought to receive it and is worthy of so bright a gift: and that the nature of the rest of the rational creation either gets the power of understanding from its natural seed, or God the Father ingrafts into it mind and understanding, as though the Son were unable to do this. In order then that God the Word, Who was in God the Father, may be clearly shewn to be both Life and Light, not of some individually, of others not, but by some ineffable mode of participation, as wisdom and understanding (which is what is called light in things rational), immingling Himself in all things that are, that the things rational may become rational, and things recipient of sense may have sense, which in no other way they could have had:----needs does he say, And the Light shineth in darkness and the darkness comprehended it not. As though he with all exactitude crieth aloud to his hearers after this sort: I said, sirs, teaching the truth with all my power, that the Life was the Light of men, not that any should suppose from these words that they who shew themselves righteous and good receive from another, as the reward of their conduct, the illumination from Him, but that ye might learn, that as the Word is Life in all things that have been made, quickening things recipient of life; so He is in them Light also, rendering things recipient of understanding and sense, what they are. For God the Father through the Son in the Spirit is all things in all. Darkness he calls the nature that lacks illumination, i. the whole originate nature. For since he calls Him the Light, to shew that the rational creation which lacks and is imparticipate thereof is other than It, he turns the force of the epithet used to the very contrary, doing this also, after my judgment, not without an aim, but considering in himself this above all, that the nature of things originate, producing nothing whatever from its own self, but receiving its whole being and well-being such as it is from its Creator, has rightly said to it, What hast thou that thou didst not receive. And since along with the rest, it has light itself also God-given, not possessing it does it receive it: but that which has not of itself light, how will it not be the contrary, or how |69 will it not be called darkness. For that the Light shineth in darkness is a credible demonstration (yea rather one following from very necessity), that the creation is darkness, the Word of God Light. For if the nature of things originate receive the Word of God by participation, as Light, or as of Light: it receives it then as itself darkness, and the Son shineth in it, as the light doth in darkness, even though the darkness know not a whit the Light. For this, I suppose, is the meaning of The darkness comprehended it not. For the Word of God shineth upon all things that are receptive of His Irradiance, and illumineth without exception things that have a nature receptive of illumining. But He is unknown of the darkness. For that which is the rational nature upon earth, I mean man, served the creature more than the Creator: it comprehended not the Light, for it knew not the Creator, the Fountain of wisdom, the beginning of understanding, the root of sense. Things originate possess nevertheless, of His love to man, the light, and are provided with the power of perception implanted concurrently with their passing into being. But we must again note here, that no argument will permit to suppose that the Son of God is originate or created, but in every way does He surpass our measure, and rise above the nature of the creature, and is wholly Other than they are and far removed as regards quality of essence, even as the light is not the same as darkness, but soothly contrary and parted by incomparable diversity into physical alieniety. Having now sufficiently gone through the method of reasoning hereupon in the foregoing, we will go on to what follows.
commentaries.content_en: Needs does the most wise Evangelist hasten to expand to us by this too that is now before us the thought expressed above. For he did not think, I suppose, that it would suffice to the hearers unto being able to think unerringly of God the Word, that He is verily the Light of men, by only saying, And the Life was the Light of men. For it was like I suppose that some would arise who should hear the things uttered without weighing them, and should moreover set forth or try to teach others also that the Word of God is indeed verily Light, but not Giver of light to all, but in whomsoever He will He infuses the light of understanding, approving him who |68 ought to receive it and is worthy of so bright a gift: and that the nature of the rest of the rational creation either gets the power of understanding from its natural seed, or God the Father ingrafts into it mind and understanding, as though the Son were unable to do this. In order then that God the Word, Who was in God the Father, may be clearly shewn to be both Life and Light, not of some individually, of others not, but by some ineffable mode of participation, as wisdom and understanding (which is what is called light in things rational), immingling Himself in all things that are, that the things rational may become rational, and things recipient of sense may have sense, which in no other way they could have had:----needs does he say, And the Light shineth in darkness and the darkness comprehended it not. As though he with all exactitude crieth aloud to his hearers after this sort: I said, sirs, teaching the truth with all my power, that the Life was the Light of men, not that any should suppose from these words that they who shew themselves righteous and good receive from another, as the reward of their conduct, the illumination from Him, but that ye might learn, that as the Word is Life in all things that have been made, quickening things recipient of life; so He is in them Light also, rendering things recipient of understanding and sense, what they are. For God the Father through the Son in the Spirit is all things in all. Darkness he calls the nature that lacks illumination, i. the whole originate nature. For since he calls Him the Light, to shew that the rational creation which lacks and is imparticipate thereof is other than It, he turns the force of the epithet used to the very contrary, doing this also, after my judgment, not without an aim, but considering in himself this above all, that the nature of things originate, producing nothing whatever from its own self, but receiving its whole being and well-being such as it is from its Creator, has rightly said to it, What hast thou that thou didst not receive. And since along with the rest, it has light itself also God-given, not possessing it does it receive it: but that which has not of itself light, how will it not be the contrary, or how |69 will it not be called darkness. For that the Light shineth in darkness is a credible demonstration (yea rather one following from very necessity), that the creation is darkness, the Word of God Light. For if the nature of things originate receive the Word of God by participation, as Light, or as of Light: it receives it then as itself darkness, and the Son shineth in it, as the light doth in darkness, even though the darkness know not a whit the Light. For this, I suppose, is the meaning of The darkness comprehended it not. For the Word of God shineth upon all things that are receptive of His Irradiance, and illumineth without exception things that have a nature receptive of illumining. But He is unknown of the darkness. For that which is the rational nature upon earth, I mean man, served the creature more than the Creator: it comprehended not the Light, for it knew not the Creator, the Fountain of wisdom, the beginning of understanding, the root of sense. Things originate possess nevertheless, of His love to man, the light, and are provided with the power of perception implanted concurrently with their passing into being. But we must again note here, that no argument will permit to suppose that the Son of God is originate or created, but in every way does He surpass our measure, and rise above the nature of the creature, and is wholly Other than they are and far removed as regards quality of essence, even as the light is not the same as darkness, but soothly contrary and parted by incomparable diversity into physical alieniety. Having now sufficiently gone through the method of reasoning hereupon in the foregoing, we will go on to what follows.
commentaries.content_pt: Necessita o mais sábio Evangelista apressar-se em expandir para nós também o pensamento expresso acima, que agora está diante de nós. Pois ele não pensou, suponho, que bastaria aos ouvintes poderem pensar de maneira correta sobre Deus o Verbo, que Ele é verdadeiramente a Luz dos homens, apenas dizendo: E a Vida era a Luz dos homens. Pois era como suponho que alguns surgiriam que deveriam ouvir as coisas proferidas sem ponderá-las, e deveriam, além disso, expor ou tentar ensinar outros também que o Verbo de Deus é de fato verdadeiramente Luz, mas não Doador de luz a todos, mas em quem quer que Ele queira, Ele infunde a luz do entendimento, aprovando aquele que |68 deve recebê-la e é digno de tão brilhante presente: e que a natureza do resto da criação racional ou obtém o poder de entendimento de sua semente natural, ou Deus o Pai injeta nele mente e entendimento, como se o Filho fosse incapaz de fazer isso. Para que então Deus o Verbo, que estava em Deus o Pai, possa ser claramente mostrado como sendo tanto Vida quanto Luz, não de alguns individualmente, de outros não, mas por algum modo inefável de participação, como sabedoria e entendimento (que é o que se chama luz nas coisas racionais), imiscindo-se em todas as coisas que são, para que as coisas racionais possam se tornar racionais, e as coisas receptivas de sentido possam ter sentido, o que de outra forma não poderiam ter:----necessita ele dizer, E a Luz brilha nas trevas e as trevas não a compreenderam. Como se ele, com toda exatidão, clamasse aos seus ouvintes desta forma: Eu disse, senhores, ensinando a verdade com todo o meu poder, que a Vida era a Luz dos homens, não que alguém devesse supor a partir dessas palavras que aqueles que se mostram justos e bons recebem de outro, como recompensa de sua conduta, a iluminação d'Ele, mas que vós possais aprender que, assim como o Verbo é Vida em todas as coisas que foram feitas, vivificando as coisas receptivas de vida; assim Ele é também neles Luz, tornando as coisas receptivas de entendimento e sentido, o que elas são. Pois Deus o Pai, através do Filho no Espírito, é todas as coisas em todos. As trevas ele chama a natureza que carece de iluminação, isto é, toda a natureza originária. Pois, uma vez que ele o chama de Luz, para mostrar que a criação racional que carece e é imparticipante disso é diferente d'Ele, ele volta a força do epíteto usado para o próprio contrário, fazendo isso também, a meu ver, não sem um objetivo, mas considerando em si mesmo isso acima de tudo, que a natureza das coisas originárias, não produzindo nada de si mesma, mas recebendo todo o seu ser e bem-estar como é de seu Criador, disse corretamente a ela: O que tens que não recebeste? E uma vez que, juntamente com o resto, tem a luz em si também, dada por Deus, não possuindo-a, a recebe: mas aquilo que não tem em si luz, como não será o contrário, ou como não será chamado de trevas? Pois que a Luz brilha nas trevas é uma demonstração credível (sim, mais bem uma que segue da própria necessidade), de que a criação é trevas, o Verbo de Deus Luz. Pois se a natureza das coisas originárias recebe o Verbo de Deus por participação, como Luz, ou como de Luz: ela o recebe então como se fosse trevas, e o Filho brilha nela, como a luz brilha nas trevas, mesmo que as trevas não conheçam nem um pouco a Luz. Pois isso, suponho, é o significado de As trevas não a compreenderam. Pois o Verbo de Deus brilha sobre todas as coisas que são receptivas de Sua Irradiação, e ilumina sem exceção as coisas que têm uma natureza receptiva de iluminar. Mas Ele é desconhecido das trevas. Pois aquilo que é a natureza racional sobre a terra, quero dizer, o homem, serviu à criatura mais do que ao Criador: não compreendeu a Luz, pois não conheceu o Criador, a Fonte da sabedoria, o princípio do entendimento, a raiz do sentido. As coisas originárias possuem, no entanto, por Seu amor ao homem, a luz, e são dotadas do poder de percepção implantado juntamente com seu passar para o ser. Mas devemos novamente notar aqui que nenhum argumento permitirá supor que o Filho de Deus é originário ou criado, mas de todas as maneiras Ele supera nossa medida, e se eleva acima da natureza da criatura, e é totalmente Outro do que elas são e muito distante em relação à qualidade da essência, assim como a luz não é a mesma que as trevas, mas verdadeiramente contrária e separada por incomparável diversidade em alienidade física. Tendo agora percorrido suficientemente o método de raciocínio a esse respeito no que precede, prosseguiremos para o que se segue.

commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: And the light shines in darkness; and the darkness overcame it not. And the light shineth, or did shine, in darkness. Many understand this, that the light of reason, which God gave to every one, might have brought them to the knowledge of God by the visible effects of his Providence in this world: but the darkness did not comprehend it, because men, blinded by their passions, would not attend to the light of reason. Or we may again understand it, with Maldonatus, of the lights of grace, against which obstinate sinners wilfully shut their eyes.
commentaries.content_en: And the light shines in darkness; and the darkness overcame it not. And the light shineth, or did shine, in darkness. Many understand this, that the light of reason, which God gave to every one, might have brought them to the knowledge of God by the visible effects of his Providence in this world: but the darkness did not comprehend it, because men, blinded by their passions, would not attend to the light of reason. Or we may again understand it, with Maldonatus, of the lights of grace, against which obstinate sinners wilfully shut their eyes.
commentaries.content_pt: E a luz brilha nas trevas; e as trevas não a venceram. E a luz brilha, ou brilhou, nas trevas. Muitos entendem isso, que a luz da razão, que Deus deu a cada um, poderia tê-los levado ao conhecimento de Deus pelos efeitos visíveis de Sua Providência neste mundo: mas as trevas não a compreenderam, porque os homens, cegos por suas paixões, não atenderiam à luz da razão. Ou podemos entender novamente, com Maldonatus, as luzes da graça, contra as quais os pecadores obstinados voluntariamente fecharam os olhos.

commentaries.author: Irenaeus of Lyons
commentaries.content: For, in discoursing of the Saviour and declaring that all things beyond the Pleroma received form from Him, he says that He is the fruit of the entire Pleroma. For he styles Him a "light which shineth in darkness, and which was not comprehended"
commentaries.content_en: For, in discoursing of the Saviour and declaring that all things beyond the Pleroma received form from Him, he says that He is the fruit of the entire Pleroma. For he styles Him a "light which shineth in darkness, and which was not comprehended"
commentaries.content_pt: Pois, ao discorrer sobre o Salvador e declarar que todas as coisas além do Pleroma receberam forma d'Ele, ele diz que Ele é o fruto de todo o Pleroma. Pois ele O chama de "luz que brilha nas trevas, e que não foi compreendida"

commentaries.author: John Chrysostom
commentaries.content: Or thus: throughout the whole foregoing passage he, had been speaking of creation; then he mentions the spiritual; benefits which the Word brought w with it: and the life was the light of men. He said not, the light of Jews, but of all men without exception; for not the Jews only, but the Gentiles also have cometo this knowledge. The Angels he omits, for he is speaking of human nature, to whom the Word came bringing glad tidings. Life having come to us, the empire of death is dissolved; a light having shone upon us, there is darkness no longer: but there remains ever a life which death, a light which darkness cannot overcome. Whence he continues, And the light shines in darkness: by darkness meaning death and error, for sensible light does not shine in darkness, but darkness must be removed first; whereas the preaching of Christ shone forth amidst the reign of error, and caused it to disappear, and Christ by dying changed death into life, so overcoming it, that, those who were already in its grasp, were brought back again. Forasmuch then as neither death nor error has overcome his light, which is every where conspicuous shilling forth by its own strength; therefore he adds, And the darkness comprehended it not.
commentaries.content_en: Or thus: throughout the whole foregoing passage he, had been speaking of creation; then he mentions the spiritual; benefits which the Word brought w with it: and the life was the light of men. He said not, the light of Jews, but of all men without exception; for not the Jews only, but the Gentiles also have cometo this knowledge. The Angels he omits, for he is speaking of human nature, to whom the Word came bringing glad tidings. Life having come to us, the empire of death is dissolved; a light having shone upon us, there is darkness no longer: but there remains ever a life which death, a light which darkness cannot overcome. Whence he continues, And the light shines in darkness: by darkness meaning death and error, for sensible light does not shine in darkness, but darkness must be removed first; whereas the preaching of Christ shone forth amidst the reign of error, and caused it to disappear, and Christ by dying changed death into life, so overcoming it, that, those who were already in its grasp, were brought back again. Forasmuch then as neither death nor error has overcome his light, which is every where conspicuous shilling forth by its own strength; therefore he adds, And the darkness comprehended it not.
commentaries.content_pt: Ou assim: ao longo de todo o trecho anterior, ele havia falado da criação; então ele menciona os benefícios espirituais que o Verbo trouxe consigo: e a vida era a luz dos homens. Ele não disse, a luz dos judeus, mas de todos os homens sem exceção; pois não apenas os judeus, mas também os gentios chegaram a este conhecimento. Os Anjos ele omite, pois está falando da natureza humana, a quem o Verbo veio trazendo boas novas. A vida tendo chegado a nós, o império da morte é dissolvido; uma luz tendo brilhado sobre nós, não há mais trevas: mas permanece sempre uma vida que a morte, uma luz que as trevas não podem vencer. Daí ele continua, E a luz brilha nas trevas: por trevas entendendo a morte e o erro, pois a luz sensível não brilha nas trevas, mas as trevas devem ser removidas primeiro; enquanto a pregação de Cristo brilhou no meio do reinado do erro, e fez com que ele desaparecesse, e Cristo, morrendo, transformou a morte em vida, superando-a, de modo que aqueles que já estavam em seu domínio foram trazidos de volta. Portanto, como nem a morte nem o erro superaram Sua luz, que é visível em toda parte brilhando por sua própria força; portanto, ele acrescenta, E as trevas não a compreenderam.

commentaries.author: Methodius of Olympus
commentaries.content: They ungratefully and malignantly ask, Who is this? as if they had never yet seen their Benefactor, and Him whom divine miracles, beyond the power of man, had made famous and renowned; for the darkness comprehended not
commentaries.content_en: They ungratefully and malignantly ask, Who is this? as if they had never yet seen their Benefactor, and Him whom divine miracles, beyond the power of man, had made famous and renowned; for the darkness comprehended not
commentaries.content_pt: Eles perguntam ingrata e malignamente, Quem é este? como se nunca tivessem visto seu Benfeitor, e Aquele que os milagres divinos, além do poder do homem, tornaram famoso e renomado; pois as trevas não compreenderam.

commentaries.author: Tatian the Assyrian
commentaries.content: In itself it is darkness, and there is nothing luminous in it. And this is the meaning of the saying, "The darkness comprehendeth not the light."
commentaries.content_en: In itself it is darkness, and there is nothing luminous in it. And this is the meaning of the saying, "The darkness comprehendeth not the light."
commentaries.content_pt: Em si mesmo, é escuridão, e não há nada luminoso nela. E este é o significado do ditado: "A escuridão não compreendeu a luz."

commentaries.author: Theophylact of Ochrid
commentaries.content: The Light, namely, the Word of God, shineth in the darkness, which means, It shines in death and in deception. Even when He Himself was dead, He so completely vanquished death that He compelled death to vomit forth those whom it had swallowed before Him. And the preaching of the Word also shines amidst the deception of the pagan Greeks. And the darkness comprehended it not. Neither death nor deception overcame it, for this Light, God the Word, is unconquerable. Some have thought that darkness means "flesh" and "life. " For the Word shone forth while in the flesh and in this life. And the darkness, that is, the opposing power of the evil one, tempted and persecuted the Light, but found It to be unassailable and invincible. Darkness means "flesh," not according to the nature of fleshâ€”far from it. â€”but because of sin. Flesh contains absolutely nothing evil when it is directed in accordance with its nature. But when directed against its nature and made to serve sin, it is called, and indeed becomes darkness.
commentaries.content_en: The Light, namely, the Word of God, shineth in the darkness, which means, It shines in death and in deception. Even when He Himself was dead, He so completely vanquished death that He compelled death to vomit forth those whom it had swallowed before Him. And the preaching of the Word also shines amidst the deception of the pagan Greeks. And the darkness comprehended it not. Neither death nor deception overcame it, for this Light, God the Word, is unconquerable. Some have thought that darkness means "flesh" and "life. " For the Word shone forth while in the flesh and in this life. And the darkness, that is, the opposing power of the evil one, tempted and persecuted the Light, but found It to be unassailable and invincible. Darkness means "flesh," not according to the nature of fleshâ€”far from it. â€”but because of sin. Flesh contains absolutely nothing evil when it is directed in accordance with its nature. But when directed against its nature and made to serve sin, it is called, and indeed becomes darkness.
commentaries.content_pt: A Luz, a saber, o Verbo de Deus, brilha nas trevas, o que significa que brilha na morte e na ilusão. Mesmo quando Ele mesmo estava morto, Ele venceu tão completamente a morte que obrigou a morte a vomitar aqueles que havia engolido antes d'Ele. E a pregação do Verbo também brilha em meio à ilusão dos pagãos gregos. E a escuridão não a compreendeu. Nem a morte nem a ilusão a superaram, pois esta Luz, Deus o Verbo, é inconquistável. Alguns pensaram que a escuridão significa "carne" e "vida." Pois o Verbo brilhou enquanto na carne e nesta vida. E a escuridão, ou seja, o poder oposto do maligno, tentou e perseguiu a Luz, mas a encontrou inabalável e invencível. A escuridão significa "carne," não de acordo com a natureza da carne—longe disso—mas por causa do pecado. A carne não contém absolutamente nada de mal quando é direcionada de acordo com sua natureza. Mas quando é direcionada contra sua natureza e feita para servir ao pecado, é chamada, e de fato se torna escuridão.
